title: `[[0]*n]*m` and `dict.fromkeys(keys, [])` share one inner object, not copies
rule: Python's sequence-repeat `*` copies references, not values: `[[0]*3]*3` makes three rows that are the SAME list object, so `grid[0][0]=9` mutates every row; `dict.fromkeys(keys, [])` likewise stores one shared list under every key. The inner `[0]*3` is fine because ints are immutable — the trap is only the outer repeat of a mutable. Build independent containers per element: `[[0]*3 for _ in range(3)]` and `{k: [] for k in keys}`.
triggers: modifying one row of a grid changes all rows
dict.fromkeys with list shares same list
appending to one key appends to all keys
shallow copy still mutates original nested list
